Now in order to use Facebook ads, we've got to pay. It costs money. Every time someone clicks, we have to pay Facebook. And upside. Well, upside is what we call the money we make from profitable Facebook ads. For example, we might spend $1,000 on ads, and we might make $3,000. And we would call the $3,000 we made as upside. So there would be $3,000 upside, and there would have been $1,000 downside because we spent that. And that means that there would be more upside than downside, which means that we make a profit of $2,000. And then downside. Downside is what we call the money we spend or lose from unsuccessful Facebook ads. Also, money we spend on successful ads. It's still downside because it costs us money. An example is we spend $1,000 on ads, and we might make $0. Well, we'd call the $1,000 we lost downside. So I'm pretty sure you get it. Money spent is downside. Money made is upside. And you want to constantly be aware of this. So it is of extreme importance that we always know our upside and downside when advertising on Facebook. And Facebook ads is no place for creative, artistic, and emotional thinking. I've seen so many people lose their shirts on Facebook because they think something looks cool or whatever, and it doesn't work. And I see so many people lose their money. So Facebook ads is a strict risk and reward game where the people who know their numbers take all. They take everything. And the people who ignore their numbers, they lose their shirts and everything else. And at all times, you must understand how much you have at risk, and you must be totally fine with losing money before making money, because that's how it works. And by this, I don't mean huge amounts, but when you're using Facebook, quite often you're spending hundreds of dollars before you see any money come in. And sometimes you're spending thousands of dollars before you see any money come in. And that's how it works. And you really have to get your head around that before you use it, because otherwise it will freak you out and you constantly will think that it's not working and taking your money, because when you're using it, you're paying before the results come back. But with Facebook ads, I mean, it is possible that all the money you're spending could just go, and you might not get anything back in return. So you always want to understand this. You want to look how much money have you got in your bank account. Then you want to look at how much money are you consistently making each month. Now, Facebook isn't a place to go if you don't have any money and if you're not making any money, because it's just going to make you poorer and it's going to freak you out. And we aim to make profit and ROI return on investment every time, however you must prepare for the worst case at all times, which is you lose everything. And the reason why I say this is because it does something to you emotionally when you prepare like this. I learned a lot of this from investing. And the worst thing you can do with investing in Facebook ads is to get emotional. And so the worst investors are emotional ones. So the last place you want to put your last $1,000 or your last $500 is Facebook ads. And you worked out that number by looking at your bank balance, looking at how much money you're making, and you came up with that number. Well, that's how we calculate our daily budget. And at all times using Facebook, you must keep upside and downside in mind, even when you create your campaigns. And now for one of my secret weapons. Now I wanna show you one of the most powerful things that I've created when it comes to using Facebook. And it's something that I call anti-fragile architecture and asymmetrical testing. And the way I think about ads and the way smart people think about investments is like this. You've got your downside and your upside. And the downside is this here where we can see these negatives and we've got this trend line going down. And you can see this is losing minus dollars down. And then we've got upside, which is above the line. And these are all the pluses. This is money that we're making. It's profit. And the nature of every single investment you're ever gonna do as a human being is that there's always gonna be downside. And that for sure it's an absolute fact there's always gonna be downside. There is not always upside. Not every investment has upside. And what you're always looking at is something called asymmetry here. And what asymmetry means is it means that both sides aren't symmetrical. So it means that one side's way bigger than the other side. And you're always looking for risk reward situations where the downside isn't that big but the upside is huge. Because if you do take the downside which is likely a lot of the time then the upside has to more than pay it back. If this downside here was the same size as this upside then it means that we would make zero dollars. And if the upside was, if the downside was bigger than the upside it means we'd lose money. And so this is what you have to think about when you're using Facebook ads. All the time you have to know what the downside is and what the potential upside is because only then are you setting it up properly so that you can actually make profit. And there's a special way that I architect these campaigns in order to do that. So anti-fragile architecture and asymmetrical testing. So when structuring our campaigns we want to have asymmetrical risk reward where risk is minimal and reward is uncapped with no limits. And to do this we run multiple variations of ads with multiple images to multiple large audiences all with the lowest budgets possible. So we set our budgets on these ad sets and these variations pretty low. I mean it's not as low as it can go because I know how low we can go and actually still make it work. But why we wanna do this is we want to have lots of different variations with a really low budget. So there's huge asymmetry here because when things work we can make millions and millions and millions but when things don't work we hard stop our losses at two grand or whatever your number is. Worse yet, we will treat red swans as blues and blues as reds. So this is the sort of messed up stuff that can happen when you don't use KPIs and you don't track stuff. Then this step two, once we've done that, is called scaling blue swans. So once we've created enough blue swans from testing, we can start scaling. And to scale, we find the most successful ad sets and we duplicate them multiple times, changing the budget and age range as variations while keeping the proven audience message image string the same. So when we have a profitable ad set, which is a blue swan, within it, there's a DNA string and it's made up of an audience, a message, and an image. And these combination of variables work together. Now what we wanna do is we wanna start duplicating these because it works, so we want to make more of them. However, we want to create just a tiny little micro variation in it. And we don't really wanna change the string because we know the string's good. So we want to just create a tiny little variation just to kind of trick the algorithm so that it thinks it's an entirely new string. And what we, our way of doing that is by changing the budget because Facebook's algorithm takes the budget into account. So if you have two ad sets at say $20, or if you have one ad set at $20 and it works, it's a blue swan, so if you duplicate it and set it at $20, well, Facebook might think that it's the same ad set and it will just treat it as if that's really one ad set, even though to you it looks like it's separated into two. But what happens is when we change the budget, it's, it falls the algorithm, it really tricks it. So if we have one at $20 and then the next one we create at $28 or $23 or whatever, just any random number that's not $20, then the algorithm puts it in a different category and it considers that a different string. But it's only slightly differentiated because it still has the same string. It's still got the same audience message image, but just the budgets change a little bit. And most people don't understand that just by changing the budget, you're changing a variation in the string. And we're also, when we duplicate it, we're changing the initial conditions. So we're exposing the string to a different environment and different initial conditions. So sometimes it's possible to actually have a profitable ad set and have a blue swan and duplicate it three times and see all three duplicate cloned copies fail and be red swans. And sometimes that can confuse people because they're like, what? I thought I found the right DNA string. How did it fail? Because each time you're duplicating it, it's not just a carbon copy, it's not a clone. You've got new initial conditions and those are more important than the ad itself. And you've also got different budgets and all of that. So each time you duplicate it, you might think you're just cloning the same thing and you're cloning the string, but the initial conditions are different and that can make a big, all the difference. Now let's talk about the big mistake everybody makes. And there's a lot of them with Facebook ads, but this one is a big one. And you'll see how blindingly obvious it is when I explain it here. So the big mistake everybody makes with Facebook ads is assuming a profitable ad set will always be a profitable ad set. So they create different ads and they're testing, then they find some that work, they turn off the ones that don't. And then what they do is they're like, yes, I've found it. So they just might increase the budget or something. And then it's working and they're like, yeah, this thing's working. And then a week goes by or whatever, maybe four days goes by more of the time. And then it stops working. And then when it stops working, they assume that the ad no longer works and they refresh the creative. Oh yes, the ad is broken. I know what happened. You know, I had this ad and it was working and now it's not working and the ad is broken. I can see how someone made that assumption, but they're wrong. And so the last thing you wanna do is like refresh the creative. When I hear people say this, I'm like, man, this person doesn't know what they're talking about. So a good example to look at is like nature to really see what the correct method for us to do is. So in nature, the environment is always changing and a genetic trait that is helpful in one environment can be harmful in another. And we can see this for like, with example, like penguins for example. Penguins genetically are made for like cold weather. That's their environment. So they've got good genetics for cold environment. However, if you put them in the desert, they don't have very good traits. So the environment, it's not really, traits aren't just good traits. Traits are only good when compared to an environment. You know, humans are good on earth, but we're not good underwater. However, fish are good underwater, they're not good on earth. The environment changes everything. And with Facebook, the environment's always changing. You know, new advertisers are coming in and they're advertising. And just because something worked at one stage doesn't mean it's gonna work in a new stage because the environment's literally changing. So because the environment is always changing, just because an ad set works right now, doesn't mean it will continue to work forever. And eventually all blue swans will die. So I've seen some blue swans like hang off for like four months, which is pretty insane. I've most ad sets don't last anywhere near that long. But you know, even those ones, those things which you think are like thoroughbred race horses, like you just like, well, this thing is working, it's been working forever. Even those, they stop working. You know, eventually all blue swans will die. And in nature, healthy genetics survive longer than unhealthy genetics. And because of this, they tend to propagate and produce offspring. So if you remember back to week four, how we talked about natural selection and all of that stuff, we talked about how, for example, there might be brown beetles and green beetles. And if there's a predator in the environment, like a bird, and it's got a preference for green beetles, then brown beetles, the green beetles are gonna get eaten first. And then so the brown beetles are gonna live longer, they're gonna have more babies and everything. So eventually there's gonna be more brown beetles than green ones. And eventually there will be no green ones at all, right? Very simple example. So if you've got the right genetics, if you're a beetle, then you're gonna live longer. And typically when they live longer, they're gonna propagate, they're gonna have offspring. And so that's what happens in nature. The healthy genetics and the preferential genetics, they tend to reproduce. And when you have a blue swan, the last thing you want to do is keep it running until it dies and then create a completely new one. And this is what everybody does. People have an ad set that works and it's profitable and they're like, yes, this thing's working. And then they just leave it until it dies and then they create a totally new thing. This is pretty mad. And while the blue swan is healthy, it should propagate and produce offspring of its own, just like how things do in nature. So when you have an ad set that's working and it's profitable, while it's healthy, working and profitable, you want to start propagating it. You want to start duplicating it, cloning it, starting to reproduce it, because you know eventually that healthy blue swan's gonna die. So while it's alive and while DNA is good and everything, you want to start duplicating it out. And that way, if you're constantly duplicating the good DNA, then you're constantly doing what organisms and everything do in nature, which is adapting to the environment and reproducing when things are good and not producing when things are bad. And that's what we're doing. We're not trying to duplicate and scale out red swans, we're just turning them off.
